title: practical english usage - Part 80
content:
He's (very) much  admired by his students, (     .. . very admired...) before it, but not very.

When a past participle is part of a passive verb, we  can put much  or very much 8  much      admired;        very     tightened Section8 Infinitives,      -ing    forms     and   Past   Participles grammar •  Section 9  Infinitives, -ing forms and Past Participles after Nouns, Verbs, etc not She left without saying goodbye. (     .. .without to say goodbye.) Note that prepositions are always followed by -ing forms.

dealt with in this Section; if in doubt, check  in a good dictionary.

adjectives are followed by which  kind of structure. The  more important cases are Unfortunately, there is no good way of knowing which  verbs, nouns and worried about getting old happy to see you an excuse for leaving the need to invest and many by preposition + -ing.

Many nouns and adjectives can also be followed by structures with infinitives, structures        after    nouns,     adjectives        and   prepositions I've just seen a man arrested.

We had our car broken into.

passive meanings are also possible.

After some verbs (e.g. get, have, hear), structures with past participles and Remember to buy coffee.

I don't remember seeing her before.

a difference of meaning.

After some verbs, more than one of these structures is possible, sometimes with Jack didn't hear me calling him.

I expected him  to say something.

Some verbs can also be followed by object + infinitive or object + -ing form.

We enjoyed visiting Edinburgh. (     We enjoyed to visit Edinburgh!) not I want to stop now. (     I want stopping now!) be used after many others.

Infinitive structures can be used after many verbs; structures with -ing forms can verb    + verb INTRODUCTION Nouns, Verbs, etc Past Participles after Section 9  Infinitives, -ing forms and grammar •
